note.soap:
S: Patient reports a tick bite with a bullseye rash present. Tick was attached for approximately 37 hours. No systemic symptoms reported. Exposure occurred in Georgia. Pregnancy is not possible.

O: Unable to perform physical exam due to phone-only encounter. Patient describes characteristic erythema migrans rash (bullseye appearance).

A: 
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease
  - Differential: 
    1. Early Lyme disease
    2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)
    3. Cellulitis or other tick-bite related skin infection
    4. Allergic reaction to tick bite
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or carditis reported.

P: 
  - Disposition: Routine follow-up with primary care provider recommended
  - Plan: Initiate empiric oral antibiotic therapy for early Lyme disease (e.g., doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-21 days) as per CDC guidance for patients with erythema migrans and tick exposure in endemic areas. Educate patient on signs of progression including fever, arthralgia, neurological symptoms. Advise to seek urgent care if systemic symptoms develop.
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to monitor for worsening symptoms such as fever, severe headache, facial palsy, joint swelling, or palpitations and to seek immediate medical attention if these occur.

Evidence (snippets used): CDC guidance recommends empiric treatment of erythema migrans in patients with tick exposure in endemic areas without waiting for serologic confirmation.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
